title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
NoteAbout this page





These are the completed solutions, runnable in your browser — no install needed. Click Run Code on any cell (run the import and data-loading cells near the top first). Edit any cell to experiment.
Looking for the blanks to fill in yourself? See the exercises page.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 1.1: Getting Started

Setting Up the Python Environment
If you haven’t already installed Python, Jupyter, and the necessary packages, there are instructions on the course repo in the README to do so here.








Exploring a dataset














Performing basic statistical functions using NumPy, Pandas, and SciPy.

Using NumPy



















Common Pitfall: np.std() defaults to the population standard deviation (ddof=0), while pandas .std() defaults to the sample standard deviation (ddof=1). If you are treating your data as a sample, pass ddof=1 to np.std() so the two agree. Mixing the defaults is a common source of slightly different numbers.



Using Pandas








Using SciPy

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 1.2: Data Summarizing

Compute and interpret measures of central tendency







Common Pitfall: .mode() returns a Series, not a single number, because a column can have more than one most-frequent value. Indexing with [0] grabs the first mode, but remember the others may exist – do not assume the mode is always unique.



Compute and interpret measures of variation

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 1.4: Sampling Distribution Generation

Common Pitfall: Do not confuse the standard deviation of the raw data with the standard deviation of the sample means (the standard error). The histogram below shows the spread of sample means, which is much narrower than the spread of the original population. By the Central Limit Theorem, the sampling distribution of the mean becomes approximately normal as the sample size grows, even when the population itself is skewed.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Exercise 1.4: Sampling Distribution Generation







Self-Check
By the end of this week, you should be able to:

Load a dataset and inspect its structure with .info() and .describe().
Compute measures of center (mean, median, mode) and spread (range, variance, standard deviation) and explain what each tells you.
Choose the sample versus population standard deviation by setting ddof correctly.
Build the five-number summary and read a box plot, histogram, and scatter plot.
Simulate a sampling distribution of the mean and describe how the Central Limit Theorem applies.
Run a basic two-sample t-test with scipy.stats and read its t-statistic and p-value.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text: Walkthrough 2.1: Advanced Plots

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Exercise 2.1: Advanced Plots













Datetime Accessor .dt:

The .dt accessor is used with pandas Series that contain datetime objects.
It provides access to datetime-specific attributes and methods.

Convert to a Period with Monthly Frequency:

The method .to_period('M') converts each datetime value into a Period object with a monthly frequency.
The argument 'M' stands for “month”. This means that each date is represented as a period corresponding to its month and year.
For example, if grading_date is 2023-03-15, converting it with .to_period('M') will result in a period like 2023-03.

Walkthrough 2.3: Data Preprocessing

Inspect the Data after Loading








Handle Missing Values








Handling Outliers

Common Pitfall: The 1.5 * IQR rule flags points as potential outliers, but a flagged value is not automatically an error. Dropping every flagged row can throw away legitimate data and bias your results. Inspect outliers before removing them, and document why you removed any.









Data Transformation








Data Visualizations on Preprocessed Data

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 2.4: Correlations

Common Pitfall: Correlation measures only the strength of a linear association, and a strong correlation does not imply that one variable causes the other. Two variables can be highly correlated because of a third, lurking variable. Also, .corr() (Pearson) can miss strong non-linear relationships, so always pair it with a scatter plot.


Correlation Matrix








Visualize Correlations








Create Scatter Plots for Meaningful Correlations

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Exercise 2.4: Correlations

Correlation Matrix








Visualize Correlations








Create Scatter Plots for Meaningful Correlations








Self-Check
By the end of this week, you should be able to:

Build advanced visualizations (correlation heatmaps, pair plots, and time series line charts) to explore relationships.
Carry out a basic exploratory data analysis: check for missing values, remove duplicates, and summarize variables.
Preprocess data by imputing missing values, handling outliers with the IQR rule, scaling features, and encoding categorical variables.
Compute and interpret a correlation matrix while remembering that correlation does not imply causation.
Explain why a scatter plot should accompany any correlation coefficient.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 3.2: t-tests

Common Pitfall: A p-value is not the probability that the null hypothesis is true. It is the probability of seeing data at least as extreme as yours if the null hypothesis were true. A small p-value is evidence against the null, not a statement about how likely the null is.


Common Pitfall: The alternative argument controls whether the test is one-tailed ('greater' or 'less') or two-tailed ('two-sided', the default). Pick the direction before you see the data, based on the question. A one-tailed test only detects an effect in the specified direction, so using the wrong tail can hide a real effect.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text: Exercise 3.2: t-tests

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 3.3: Comparative Tests

The *groups syntax unpacks the lists from the groups variable so that each list is passed as a separate argument to stats.f_oneway. This function then performs a one-way ANOVA test across the different groups.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text: Exercise 3.3: Comparative Tests

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Walkthrough 3.4: Non-Parametric Tests

Common Pitfall: Non-parametric tests like Mann-Whitney U and Kruskal-Wallis do not assume normally distributed data, which makes them useful for skewed data such as salaries. But they compare distributions (ranks/medians) rather than means, so do not report them as tests of the difference in means. Reach for them when the normality assumption of a t-test or ANOVA is clearly violated.

title: Solutions — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Exercise 3.4: Non-Parametric Tests













Self-Check
By the end of this week, you should be able to:

Simulate common distributions (binomial, normal, Poisson, exponential) and recognize their typical shapes.
Run one-sample and two-sample t-tests and choose a one-tailed or two-tailed alternative appropriately.
Interpret a p-value correctly as evidence against the null hypothesis, not as the probability the null is true.
Apply ANOVA and the chi-square test of independence to compare groups and categorical variables.
Choose non-parametric tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kruskal-Wallis) when the assumptions of parametric tests are violated.

title: Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text: Welcome! This site lets you run all of the course code directly in your browser. Python runs locally on your own machine via Pyodide (WebAssembly) — there is nothing to install, no account to create, and no cloud server involved.

title: Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
Two ways to work


📝 Exercises
The live-coding template. Code cells start mostly empty so you can fill them in as we go. Your edits are saved automatically in this browser, so you can pick up where you left off.


✅ Solutions
The completed code for every walkthrough and exercise. Run any cell to see the output, or edit a cell to experiment with your own variations.

title: Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
How it works

Click Run Code on any code cell to execute it in your browser.
Run the import cell and the data-loading cells near the top of a page first, then work top to bottom — later cells reuse variables from earlier ones.
The first cell you run takes a few seconds while Python and the data packages load. After that it’s fast.
The two datasets — data_dev_survey.csv (walkthroughs) and coffee_quality.csv (exercises) — are bundled with the page, so pd.read_csv(...) just works.







NoteNeed the originals?



The Jupyter notebooks, slides, and data are all in the course GitHub repository. You can also run the notebooks on Google Colab or Binder if you prefer a full Jupyter environment.








TipBrowser support



The in-browser plots need a modern browser (Chrome, Edge, Firefox, or Safari, kept up to date). If a plot cell shows an OffscreenCanvas error, update your browser to the latest version.

title: Walkthroughs & Exercises — Fundamentals of Statistics with Python
text:
TipHow to use this page





Everything runs in your browser — there’s no Python or Jupyter to install.

Click Run Code on the import cell and the data-loading cells near the top first.
Then work through the page top to bottom, filling in each cell as we live-code together.
Your edits are saved automatically in this browser. Use Start Over on a cell to reset it.
The very first run takes a few seconds while Python loads in the background.

Stuck? Open the completed solutions in another tab.
